titleStmt.title: Letter from Marie Martin to her mother, Mary Martin, 7 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Marie Martin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Marie Martin (1892–1975) joined the Voluntary Aid Detachment (VAD) upon the outbreak of World War 1. During her time as a VAD in Malta, and at the Battle of the Somme in France, she regularly wrote to her mother Mary Martin (1866-1955) in Dublin. In her letters, she tended to describe her day to day duties as a VAD, her patients and trips she would take on her days off. In this letter, Marie begins by describing her ailments to her mother. She is suffering from diarrhoea and her face is swollen from insect bites. She discusses the number of patients she is caring for and some of her day to day duties as a VAD in Malta.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
7th Nov 1915 My own dearest Mother,< I am very sorry for leaving you so
long without writing but really I have
not had time till to-day when matron
gave me a day off as she did not
think I looked up to the mark, for
the last couple of days I have had a
touch of diarrhoea which rather disturbed
my nights rest & instead of the work getting
easier it is getting harder. I have been changed
up to upper E Block & we got in a new
convoy the other day with a very bad lot
of dysentery cases but I am glad to say the
days rest has done me a lot of good & I
am feeling quite myself again & in the
best of form, it is no wonder the Matron
said I was not looking well, my face
is like a bit of putty pulled in to a very
funny shape as the mosquitos and sandflies
have my face in a terrible state all my
eyes swollen up with them, it is very
funny how they go for me, the men
have a great joke about it & say they
the mosquitos know whats good" I suppose you
got my Cath about the camera, they say it
is much better to buy one out here as one
has to declare everything in parcels & would
most probably take camera out I shall try & write you a proper description
of this sometime when I can get a man
to bring it home & post it in England, as
it is rather like the G. Prisoners here
we have to be very careful what we
say or the letters do not go, I shall
explain all this later . I am really not lonely as we have no
time to be, you might tell Aunt Lily the
work is much harder than you can
imagine & to be sure not to come out
without camp kit wear if you are not
under canvas one very often has to use
the beds & wash basins. There is a very
strong rumour that shortly we are all
going to be sent on to Servia, I really
do not think it can be harder than
here & the sanitary arrangements could
not be much worse, it would mostly
be surgical work out there .
However we must only wait & see perhaps if you
would rather I did not go I could remain here
and go to another hospital if they are keeping
any open but what they say is that they
will not be using this much if they are
giving up Gallipoli for the present .
Letters take so long going & coming one
has to write about things long before
we really know, the post arrived to-day
but got nothing but a letter from
Nana. Tell her I was delight to get
it & hope Andrew is better, give them
all my fondest love & big kisses .
I cannot tell you how relieved I was
to hear the good news of Charlie, I
hope he will continue to let us know
how things are going with him I do
not think they are doing much out
there we hear no war news except
now & again a bad rumour of a
Transport being sunk — It is quite
exciting here some times, the practicing
of the guns make quite a good noise
we thought we were being invaded
when we 1st came. I would like so much to be able to
do something for the men at Xmas in
my flock! but of course the difficulty
is to know what to get them sent
out, so I was think perhaps if Violet
made a small collection round our
relations & friends & collected enough
of pounds I could give them a
little treat by give them cakes &
cigarettes the poor men have a very
hard time of it here. That is all
I can say — I am sure they would
not mind giving about 5/- each, the
other V,A.D is doing the same for
our 140 beds as we try & give
them as jolly a day as possible .
What about your photo, I am looking
for it, Everyone of the Girls said you
looked so sweet at the train & they were
no surprised at me feeling leaving you
so much. Did the Oxfordshire go to Dublin
since, I wonder if you are getting my
—letters alright, I never got the things
from the J A N Stores it was nearly
a £1 worth — but perhaps they will
turn up, the post is very bad we
long from Sunday to Sunday because
that is the day we get the post out
here. Last night a convoy of Pam Nurses
arrived just for the week and their
uniform is too smart for words & then they
have stars just the same as officers on
their shoulders with a sand brown leather
belt & a pale blue uniform with white
aprons & square bibs I have a very nice lot of men but was
very sorry having lower E. I was just
getting in to their ways & to know the
patients, however all these things are in
the days work. I hope this will find your cold alright
& that Andrew is up and well
With every good wish love & blessing
to you dearest Mother Your ever loving daughter Marie
